perturbation.original_text: Wade will be solely responsible for withholding and paying any and all federal, state and local taxes, including but not limited to payroll, unemployment, social security and income taxes and any other payments which may be owed by Wade as a result of or in connection with payments made by Naked for Wade Services rendered under this Agreement. Wade acknowledges that he is not qualified for and will not receive any Naked employment benefits or other incidents of employment as a result of the Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Wade will be solely responsible for paying income taxes. Wade acknowledges that he is not qualified for and will not receive any Naked employment benefits or other incidents of employment as a result of the Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By narrowing Wade's tax obligations to 'income taxes' only, it creates ambiguity and contradicts the broader responsibilities of independent contractors, who are generally responsible for self-employment taxes (Social Security and Medicare).
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts 26 U.S.C. § 1402, which defines 'self-employment income' as gross income derived by an individual from any trade or business carried on by such individual, less deductions allowed. The changed text suggests Wade is only responsible for income tax, neglecting his obligations for self-employment tax (Social Security and Medicare) as an independent contractor, leading to potential tax law violations.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 26 U.S. Code § 1402 - Definitions | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 26 U.S. Code § 1402 - Definitions U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Net earnings from self-employment The term “ net earnings from self-employment ” means the gross income derived by an individual from any trade or business carried on by such individual, less the deductions allowed by this subtitle which are attributable to such trade or business , plus his distributive share (whether or not distributed) of income or loss described in section 702(a)(8) from any trade or business carried on by a partnership of which he is a member; except that in computing such gross income and deductions and such distributive share of partnership ordinary income or loss— (1) there shall be excluded rentals from real estate and from personal property leased with the real estate (including such rentals paid in crop shares, and including payments under section 1233(a)(2) of the Food Security Act of 1985 ( 16 U.S.C. 3833(a)(2) ) to individuals receiving benefits under section 202 or 223 of the Social Security Act ) together with the deductions attributable thereto, unless such rentals are received in the course of a trade or business as a real estate dealer; except that the preceding provisions of this paragraph shall not apply to any income derived by the owner or tenant of land if (A) such income is derived under an arrangement, between the owner or tenant and another individual, which provides that such other individual shall produce agricultural or horticultural commodities (including livestock, bees, poultry, and fur-bearing animals and wildlife) on such land, and that there shall be material participation by the owner or tenant (as determined without regard to any activities of an agent of such owner or tenant) in the production or the management of the production of such agricultural or horticultural co
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts 26 U.S.C. § 1402 because it narrows Wade's tax obligations to 'income taxes' only, neglecting his obligations for self-employment tax (Social Security and Medicare) as an independent contractor. The original text stated Wade was responsible for all federal, state, and local taxes. The scraped law snippets, particularly snippet 2, define 'net earnings from self-employment' as gross income derived from a trade or business, which includes self-employment taxes. By limiting the obligation to income tax only, the modified text deviates from the statutory definition and creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Nothing contained in this Agreement shall be deemed or construed to place the Parties in the relationship of partners, joint venturers, principal-agents, or employer-employee, it being understood that the Parties are and will remain independent contractors in all respects and neither Party shall have any right to obligate or bind the other in any manner whatsoever.
perturbation.changed_text: Nothing contained in this Agreement shall be deemed or construed to place the Parties in the relationship of partners, joint venturers, or employer-employee, it being understood that the Parties may operate in a principal-agent relationship as required for Naked's promotional needs and neither Party shall have any right to obligate or bind the other in any manner whatsoever beyond those promotional activities.
perturbation.explanation: This clause initially asserts the parties are independent contractors. However, adding the statement 'the Parties may operate in a principal-agent relationship as required for Naked's promotional needs' directly contradicts the initial statement and independent contractor status. A principal-agent relationship implies a level of control and responsibility that is inconsistent with independent contractor status.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 29 CFR § 516.2 - Employees Subject to Minimum Wage and Overtime Provisions
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification implies that Wade could be considered an employee under the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA) if the principal-agent relationship results in Naked having significant control over Wade's actions. 29 CFR § 516.2 requires employers to keep records of employees, which they would not do if they consider Wade a contractor. This conflicting description creates confusion about Wade's employment status and potential FLSA violations.

perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states that the parties are independent contractors and will remain so. The changed text introduces a principal-agent relationship for promotional needs, which contradicts the initial assertion of independent contractor status. While scraped law snippet 2 is incomplete, snippet 1 refers to 'Employees subject to minimum wage or minimum wage and overtime provisions,' implying a distinction between employees and independent contractors, a distinction blurred by the modified text. The law explanation highlights potential FLSA violations due to the conflicting employment status, further emphasizing the contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All rights to the use of the names, trademarks, service marks, symbols, logos, domain names, trade secrets, confidential know-how, patents, copyrights, any pending applications with respect to any of the foregoing, and any other intellectual property and related proprietary rights, interests and protections ("Intellectual Property Rights") in connection with Wade Products will be jointly owned by Wade and Naked.
perturbation.changed_text: All rights to the use of the names, trademarks, service marks, symbols, logos, domain names, trade secrets, confidential know-how, patents, copyrights, any pending applications with respect to any of the foregoing, and any other intellectual property and related proprietary rights, interests and protections ("Intellectual Property Rights") in connection with Wade Products will be managed by Naked, with all revenues from the Wade Products split evenly between Wade and Naked.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'jointly owned' to 'managed by Naked, with revenue split evenly' alters the fundamental ownership of the intellectual property. While revenue is shared, the control and ultimate ownership rights are shifted entirely to Naked, contradicting the concept of joint ownership.
perturbation.law_explanation: By stating that Naked manages the IP and splits revenue but not explicitly defining joint ownership, this change contradicts 17 U.S.C. § 201, which governs copyright ownership. Under copyright law, joint ownership grants specific rights and responsibilities to each owner. This modification implies Naked has unilateral control, which negates the co-ownership aspect required for 'joint works' under copyright law, potentially leading to disputes regarding IP rights, licensing, and enforcement.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 U.S. Code § 201 - Ownership of copyright | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 17 U.S. Code § 201 - Ownership of copyright U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Initial Ownership.— Copyright in a work protected under this title vests initially in the author or authors of the work. The authors of a joint work are coowners of copyright in the work. (b) Works Made for Hire.— In the case of a work made for hire, the employer or other person for whom the work was prepared is considered the author for purposes of this title, and, unless the parties have expressly agreed otherwise in a written instrument signed by them, owns all of the rights comprised in the copyright. (c) Contributions to Collective Works.— Copyright in each separate contribution to a collective work is distinct from copyright in the collective work as a whole, and vests initially in the author of the contribution. In the absence of an express transfer of the copyright or of any rights under it, the owner of copyright in the collective work is presumed to have acquired only the privilege of reproducing and distributing the contribution as part of that particular collective work, any revision of that collective work, and any later collective work in the same series. (d) Transfer of Ownership.— (1) The ownership of a copyright may be transferred in whole or in part by any means of conveyance or by operation of law, and may be bequeathed by will or pass as personal property by the applicable laws of intestate succession. (2) Any of the exclusive rights comprised in a copyright, including any subdivision of any of the rights specified by section 106, may be transferred as provided by clause (1) and owned separately. The owner of any particular exclusive right is entitled, to the extent of that right, to all of the protection and remedies accorded to the copyright owner by this title. (e) Involuntary Transfer.— When an i
perturbation.justification: The modification contradicts copyright law, specifically concerning joint ownership of intellectual property. The original text explicitly states "Intellectual Property Rights will be jointly owned by Wade and Naked." The changed text alters this by stating the IP will be "managed by Naked, with all revenues from the Wade Products split evenly between Wade and Naked." This change shifts control and potentially the ultimate ownership of the IP solely to Naked, undermining the initial agreement of joint ownership. 17 U.S.C. § 201, as stated in both snippets, asserts that 'The authors of a joint work are coowners of copyright in the work.' By giving Naked managerial control, the modification effectively negates Wade's rights as a co-owner, which contradicts the legal principle of co-ownership in copyright law.
